sentences.text: He said, that engineer said to me, I don't want you to die here.
Because sooner or later you gonna die from hunger.
And they don't care if people die because they can bring in other people from other country they-- they conquered.
So-- I said, what can I do-- he asked me, what can you do?
I said I can make quilts.
Cotton, with the inside cotton, the quilts.
He said, all right.
I will see to it that you can open a factory of quilts.
Because we had a factory-- that boss of ours had a factory for quilts, and I gave them the material to make those quilts.
And I took back the quilts ready.
And I saw how they making the quilts.
I am very handy-- when I see something-- somebody do something, I can do it myself, too.
And I said, I can make quilts.
And he sent me-- he went to the personnel.
He asked me, or I have something work-- while they should give me a recommendation.
They will give you a recommendation, and you going to go and make-- help out my factory on quilts, and you gonna work there.
And sure enough, I gave him a golden watch I had from Poland with me, a golden watch.
I gave him that golden watch away, should save my life.
And he went there to the-- no, I-- I had it with me, but he said you're going to give it to that man, that watch.
And he says, you go there and to the personnel-- he told me where it is-- and you're going to ask for this and this man.
And if he going to say I am this and this man, then you take the watch and give it to him.
Then-- you're not going to give it to a stranger, and he will give you that recommendation.
You're going to go to the railroad, and you're going to go out with the railroad to Donbas.
They gave me a-- to a recommendation to Donbas.
This was in Ukrainian, not far from Rovno.
Polish Rovno.
And I-- they-- I should go to the party from the Communists, and they will give you-- make everything what you need.
You showed them what you need, and they going to give it to-- make it for you.
Fact is I did that and--
No, he said, don't talk to nobody about it.
Nobody should know about it, that-- that you going, you're leaving, nobody don't.
Just go, the least people going to see you, then you're going to be better off.
And don't tell nobody where you going, what you're going to do, anything, nothing.
Go and go there.
I went there, I went to the party.
Right by the railroad, with the railroad, I got a ticket.
It's Donbas.
This is not far from Rovno.
And I was working there.
I opened a factory.
I was working-- of course, there was party people.
They wanted quilts for themself.
I made it for them, quilts, you know.
And what I care-- I say, as long I have a job and I have what to eat, and that's all.
They gave me where to live.
They-- everything-- at the party, they have everything, whatever you need, they have it.
If they don't have it, they getting.
There's no such thing, they cannot have it.
Anyway, I was working there a few months, and then the Germans came to-- and they took over already, Ukrainian.
And I, at night, they gave me a-- a rifle, I should go and watch, at the rifle.
If I see somebody, he hit somebody or that, I should shoot them.
That's what they told me.
I never had a rifle in my hands.
They gave me a rifle to go in the streets and watch the streets.
So I was thinking to myself, what I'm going to do?
They going to catch me, and I escape them, they going to kill me.
So I have to escape-- escape them.
I have to go away from them.
I went on again on the railroad.
And I went up to Asia, Kazakhstan.
And when I came to Kazakhstan, Alma-Ata-- there called the town.
I just went-- I said-- I didn't buy no ticket.
I went on the train, I made believe I cannot talk to them in their language, wherever in Russian.
I made believe that I don't know nothing.
And they-- they were asking me, I-- I looked at them and I, like, I don't understand what they saying to me.
And they let me go.
So I went to--
Because you must have to look like a little boy.
I did look-- I still am short, but I was shorter that time.
Anyway I went to Alma-Ata, I went to the Communist party.
Again, I came here, a refugee, and I'm looking to work.
They send me to a tabac-- a-- a-- company, tabac, fabrique.
And I was working in that fabrique a while.
And then, they came and they took me in a [NON-ENGLISH]-- [NON-ENGLISH] is about-- this means the working-- working-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] working party, the-- you know.
Then they send me to Siberia.
They send me to Siberia.
They gave me an iron.
I had to cover my nose, my ears, because they would freeze up.
It was 40 to 60 degrees below zero.
And they had people there without noses, without ears because they freezed up.
They didn't know.
And they-- and I-- they told me I should cover my nose and my ears, and I did.
And they gave me an iron to dig.
And you can dig iron with iron-- you can.
There's nothing, I couldn't do anything.
And they led-- they was asking who was mechanics.
I picked up my hand-- I'm a mechanic.
I have no choice, I have to do something.
To go inside, to work not in this-- outside.
And they send me to a-- a-- from the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from the war-- from the-- they send me to work there, and make the-- a ammunition.
And I went there, and I am very handy and I see how they working.
And I said, they should show me, because I worked other things, and I don't know those things to work.
They showed it to me, and I was working there for a few years-- till they freed Poland.
And they freed Poland and I went back--
No, well, there was no correspondence with the-- with-- yes.
Even if we wrote them a letter, they never got it, the letter.
They look what we are writing.
So we don't write anything against the government from Russia, you know.
They were-- since it was so big that they-- it was unbelievable.
And they didn't know where I am, because they didn't get no writing from me.
We couldn't talk.
We didn't-- they didn't allow us to talk to each other something of work, anything about the work, about the food, about the anything that is bad.
They didn't allow us.
We shouldn't organize ourselves.
You know, we had [NON-ENGLISH] there, what they were among us, and they believe that they also were-- they ask me I should become a [NON-ENGLISH], and I said I cannot do that.
I should-- I should find out what any-- anybody says bad, or I should say it's good.
If they brought new people, I should agitate them that they are going to have good stay in here.
I said, I'm sorry I cannot do that, because my conscious doesn't allow me.
Because I knew what the real life what we had there.
That it's bad, it's good, but I am going to say it is good-- the opposite.
So I couldn't.
We-- we-- what's your name, and where you coming, just things.
And not about food, and not about the work, nothing.
No.
No, they were-- I-- I already was so upset with myself, where I am, and wind up to be in Siberia, and now to be afraid to go outside if I should freeze off.
It's-- it terrible.
I know about the war, because in 1939, I read their papers were arriving, or the radio was-- they didn't have television or radios.
They had-- they said that the war-- they took this city, this city.
They-- they always advertised.
We had no newspaper, we couldn't get it, the newspaper.
But they brought in newspapers to sell.
Anybody could get it-- 20 newspapers, who buys 20 newspapers at a time?
I ask, why-- what they gonna do with 20 newspapers?
They said, excuse me.
It's their discretion-- it's for toilet paper.
We didn't have no toilet paper, so they buying that newspaper to use as toilet paper.
Toilet paper it was.
They made barracks for us.
They made the barracks.
They cut it out, a piece of a mountain, and they with-- boards, two by four.
You know, boards.
And they made a barrack where we were sleeping.
The water was running down from the-- those walls.
And I was afraid I shouldn't get sick from-- from this.
That's why I wanted to get out there, fast.
I said, I'm a mechanic.
I shouldn't be outside, and I should have where to sleep.
And finally, they took me, they send me to Novosibirsk.
There was the factory, and I was working there all day.
This must've been in 1943, 1942, '43.
The war was-- kept going on, yeah.
We know about everything, what they doing-- but not complete, like they killing people.
We didn't know about it.
But I didn't trust those Germans.
Anyway, I was working there.
They gave me where to sleep, and a nice [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] house there, with a lot of people in the one room.
We were sleeping there.
Only boys, or a separate woman's.
From all over.
From Romania, from the-- Austria, from Czechoslovakia, from Poland.
You name it, and from all of those countries they took it in Latvia, Lithuania, Estonia-- they took over, and they send them into the coal mines.
No, all of the religions.
Most Jewish, mostly Jewish-- because the Polak, they didn't have but to run away from it.
You understand?
The Polak, or the Lithuanian, most the Jewish people who are seeing what is going on, they escaped them.
Anyway-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] so-- where was--
To work in that factory.
There I got to know my wife, she was always alone, by herself.
Ida Wojslowitzr.
W-O-J-S-L-O-W-I-T-Z-R.
She was working at another factory.
And I met her and then when we got together, no marriage, no nothing, but we got together because there was nothing like here, they get married, did something, nothing.
This was when I was in Siberia, in-- the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the fields, you know.
We couldn't talk at that time.
But here, we were free already, more free, that we could talk to each other.
And finally, somebody told me that there's a girl, she's alone, you're alone, and maybe you can get together or something.
And I went to her, and I-- I-- bought her presents.
And-- because while I was working, I got her to the--
My first.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and finally, her-- there, the place where she was staying, she was-- by a couple there, but they had their house, she was staying with them.
And they like-- got to like me very much, because I was caring for her, and I brought her some food.
So they gave me her quilt, and a pillow, and everything.
Take it to your place where you're staying.
And I-- and I took it, what I said, and she had no choice to come to me.
[LAUGHS] Now what year was this, Sam?
This was in 1944.
Yeah.
And-- cause--
With the Russians, yeah.
I was still with the Russians.
And then--
Oh I was on machines.
What-- I ran machine, and we were tying up their parts.
And we had to cut it, with a knife that was turning around, and the knife was cutting till it was very nice and clean and nice and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] smooth.
Anyway, and all the different things.
Whatever they show me to do, I did it.
And if it came up, and then I got to-- go to the other factory, machines, and I was working leather machines.
It was no such thing I should stay not doing anything.
I always had work.
And I was a good worker, so they treat me good, too, so they give me extra food.
I ate three meals.
Anyway, I got to know my wife.
And then when they--
Yeah, we got together and we were living together.
We helped each other.
With food-- that's the most important thing.
I could bring her a piece of bread, and I could bring her anything, you know-- and she brought for me.
She was growing potatoes, her own potatoes, and she gave me a bowl of potatoes.
I didn't need it, but I made her believe OK, I take it.
But I-- I gave her this, other things.
Anyway, we-- we were good to each other and everything, and then when it came--
Yeah, because I had somebody to talk to, and to share everything.
You know, it's a different life then.
And we became so like we would be like one person-- so close with each other.
And then when they freed already Poland.
Yeah, when they freed Ukrainian, I took her to-- to Poltava.
Poltava, this is in Russia, yet, Ukrainian there.
Closer to Poland.
Oh, by trains.
Oh, by trains.
And I always find to go-- I had money to pay for her ticket at that time, and I went there.
I went there and I--
No, we couldn't go just by ourself.
We had to give under the table to get recommendations otherwise they wouldn't let us go.
I had because they paid me already because I was working on the factory.
They paid me.
We had to give them something to-- they should-- bribe them, that's all.
Anyway, I came to Poltava, I went there-- again to the party.
I knew already the party can find me a job.
And they find me a job to deliver bread from the bakery to the-- to the stores-- all the stores who were-- they were selling the bread.
So I delivered the bread, so I had, of course, bread enough to eat.
And this was a pleasure, already.
Anyway, I was there a while.
And then they freed Poland.
I said we're going to go to Poland back.
And we went back to Poland, because at that time we could go already, because the Polish, they had an agreement that we should leave and come back to Poland.
No.
We were living in a room from somebody's house.
She wasn't, I--I didn't think as different.
The paper wouldn't-- I couldn't get no paper.
I couldn't do anything.
So I-- I kept her like she's my real life, and she kept me like I'm her real husband.
More than the paper.
Anyway, we simply were staying till she-- and I did for her anything is possible to keep her up, and going.
Keep her going.
Her family were in Tashkent, over-- this is Uzbekistan.
This is another part of Russia.
It was her father.
The mother was with sisters, remained in Belz.
She was Polish, from Belz.
You remember the Rabbi Belz?
The song [NON-ENGLISH]?
So she was from there.
Her mother, with other children, remained there.
And they, of course, got killed, all of them.
The father, he was in Tashkent with her sister and two brothers.
They went-- and they want to go back to Poland, by-- because they-- they couldn't stay there no more.
They couldn't take it.
They wanted to go back to the mother.
So one, they took to the army, and the other got killed.
The other brother got killed.
With her, yeah.
This was in 1944.
I went back to Poland, and I was in Rickenbach.
Yeah, I was-- then-- I was in Poland for a while.
Then I saw that anti-Semitism is still there.
Because where we're going Jewish people they start up with us, they want to fight us, you know?
So I thought.
I better escape them.
I don't have-- so I went from Poland to Czechoslovakia, from Czechoslovakia, I went to Austria, from Austria I went to Germany.
I went--when I came to Poland I went to my town, to Pinczow.
And to find out any child that remain alive.
Over 300 people were in our family, and I don't-- I don't know if-- one.
And I went to find anybody.
He had-- he had--
I didn't find nobody.
Not even one person, nobody.
They got gassed up-- they had-- in the gas chambers, and they got killed like this, with the rifles, you know.
They killed, they didn't care, they killed Jews.
Yeah, they buried them alive.
They dig out holes, and they-- and they buried them together with other people.
You have no idea what was going on at Poland.
I met one Jew what was living in Pinczow, and I was sleeping in his house.
And he warned me, don't go in the street.
If you're going to go in the street, they going to kill you.
I'm sure they're going to kill you, because they will think that you came home to take away their property.
And their property was what belongs to your family.
And they-- they going to kill you.
They killed plenty already.
And he told me that.
And I didn't even go in the street.
And I said, do you know?
He don't know.
I asked where my family, or if he knows anybody.
No.
Anyway, I was a day, one day before they made the program in Kielce, in our-- in our state, from Pinczow, the state.
A night and a day later, if I would be there, I would be killed.
And they killed a lot of Jewish people who were there in that house.
And just Poles, not the Germans, the Poles.
And I went back to Rickenbach.
I was living in Rickenbach.
With my wife.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and then when we decided we-- yeah, my wife got pregnant and she had a daughter.
She was born there in that camp, in-- in Rickenbach.
This wasn't like a camp, that Rickenbach.
But Jewish people, they had here so many houses.
And there, and another place, again so many houses.
And we belong all to the Jewish community.
Sylvia-- Sarah, after my mother, but we called her Sylvia.
Listen, I went back already.
I know they freed Poland and I'm going to go back to Poland and we going to go on living.
So that's why.
I saw what's going on that's going to be the same thing with Poland, but it was before the war.
In that camp, yeah.
We had there food.
At least we had food and we had a roof over the head.
You understand?
In the hospital.
Yeah, there was already.
What was I doing?
I was trying to handle in the black market, to make some money.
I got getting from the bowers some eggs and chickens, whatever they had for sale, potatoes.
Whatever they sell.
And I paid them the money, and sell them for more money in the bazaar, they were going.
And then I was selling there in bazaar, and the stuff, and I was making money in that time.
That's why I could afford already a child.
So you understand?
So that's why-- and then, we went-- me, my wife, and the daughter, we went to Czechoslovakia to Austria, to Germany.
And you had money then?
I had some money, yeah already, in that time.
Where?
With me.
With me.
Excuse me.
You, excuse me, the expression, I will tell you-- I had paper money.
I made the paper money with big, big bills, and I put it in my behind, inside.
Yeah, inside-- I put it in.
And when I went and crossed the-- the border.
Because there they were taking away from us everything what we had.
That would be safe.
No, I am telling you the honest truth, that is what I did.
I have no choice, I have to tell you.
Anyway, and went there in Rickenbach.
